refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from mission context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Atomicity requires separating these concerns. CR-NGHE-005.1 focuses on the derivation activity—the logical transformation from mission inputs to performance targets—which is solution-neutral and testable. Recording and management are separate concerns addressed in sub-FRs.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Provide a worksite mission scenario (e.g., excavation task with cycle-time and fuel-efficiency targets); verify that the system produces a documented set of performance requirements that are traceable to the input mission parameters and satisfy acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to worksite mission inputs.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall manage performance requirement changes in accordance with worksite safety plan and operational policies.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Capture worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) via structured intake form; parse into a canonical context model; apply a rule-based inference engine to map context attributes to performance requirement templates; generate and version-control the resulting performance targets document.

DP-B: Establish a bidirectional traceability matrix linking worksite inputs (safety plan sections, policy clauses, operational parameters) to performance targets; maintain this matrix as a living artifact; use change-tracking on both sides to detect and propagate updates; generate performance targets as a derived view of the matrix.

DP-C: Decompose worksite mission context into discrete requirement categories (safety, availability, accuracy, latency, environmental); assign each category a dedicated derivation workflow (e.g., safety → hazard analysis → performance floor); aggregate workflows into a unified performance target set; version each workflow independently.

DP-D: Implement a parametric performance model that accepts worksite context variables (mission type, site hazards, operational tempo, equipment constraints) as inputs; compute performance targets algorithmically from the model; store model coefficients and baseline calibration points; enable historical replay and sensitivity analysis.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Establish a bidirectional traceability matrix linking worksite inputs (safety plan sections, policy clauses, operational parameters) to performance targets; maintain this matrix as a living artifact; use change-tracking on both sides to detect and propagate updates; generate performance targets as a derived view of the matrix.

potential_sources: Traceability matrix design is decoupled from the specific format or storage technology of worksite inputs (safety plan, policies); the matrix abstraction remains valid whether inputs are documents, databases, or APIs.
Change-tracking mechanism is independent of performance target generation logic; either can evolve without forcing redesign of the other.
Versioning and history preservation are orthogonal to the derivation rules; audit trail can be implemented separately from the inference logic.
Minor risk: If worksite input schemas are highly volatile or poorly standardized, matrix maintenance overhead could increase; mitigated by defining a canonical input schema early.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B avoids tight coupling to specific inference algorithms (DP-A) or parametric models (DP-D), making it more resilient to changes in derivation logic. It also supports both forward traceability (context → targets) and backward traceability (targets → context), enabling impact analysis when policies or mission context change. DP-C introduces unnecessary workflow fragmentation; DP-A and DP-D are more tightly coupled to specific computational paradigms.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies) can be parsed into a structured, canonical schema. Risk: If inputs are unstructured or inconsistently formatted, matrix population becomes manual and error-prone. Mitigation: Define and validate input schema with stakeholders early; provide intake templates.

Assumption: Traceability matrix can be maintained as a single source of truth without version conflicts. Risk: Concurrent edits or distributed ownership could introduce inconsistencies. Mitigation: Implement version control (Git-like) and conflict resolution protocols; assign clear ownership.

Assumption: Change-tracking on worksite inputs will reliably trigger re-derivation of affected performance targets. Risk: Missed or delayed propagation could leave targets stale relative to mission context. Mitigation: Implement automated change detection and notification; require explicit sign-off on target updates.

Assumption: Performance targets derived from context are sufficient to satisfy acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, versioned). Risk: Derivation logic itself may be incorrect or incomplete, leading to invalid targets. Mitigation: Validate derived targets against domain expertise and historical baselines; include peer review in derivation workflow.

Assumption: History of baselines and calibration updates can be captured within the traceability matrix structure. Risk: Matrix may become unwieldy if historical depth is large. Mitigation: Implement time-series storage for baselines; link matrix to historical archive rather than embedding all history inline.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005.1 (refined): The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context by maintaining a bidirectional traceability matrix that links worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) to performance targets; the system shall track changes on both sides of the matrix, automatically propagate updates, and preserve a versioned history of performance baselines and calibration events. Acceptance criteria: (1) Traceability matrix is documented and version-controlled; (2) Every performance target is traceable to at least one worksite input; (3) Every worksite input change triggers a change-impact analysis and optional re-derivation; (4) Historical baselines and calibration updates are retrievable and auditable; (5) Derivation logic and results are peer-reviewed before release.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for safety plan traceability, performance target derivation, change tracking, and bidirectional matrix maintenance across worksite inputs and operational constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational parameters and worksite input updates (safety plan sections, policy clauses) that feed into the traceability matrix.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits performance target violations and change propagation notifications when matrix updates detect misalignment between inputs and derived targets.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational state data to validate performance targets against actual system behavior and detect drift requiring matrix updates.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of safety plan traceability, performance target derivation, and change tracking. The bidirectional matrix is inherently a safety governance artifact. Minor diffusion: Tasking interface must push input updates; Telemetry must push validation data; Vehicle/Teleoperation consume derived targets via SafetyAlert.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator, not a domain-specific safety artifact owner. The traceability matrix would be scattered across multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Change tracking and matrix maintenance would diffuse across multiple modules, violating cohesion.

Very poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational asset, not a governance or traceability layer. Safety plan sections, policy clauses, and performance target derivation are orthogonal to vehicle control logic. High diffusion: matrix would need to be replicated or accessed remotely, creating tight coupling to external safety logic.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a safety governance module. The bidirectional traceability matrix is not a teleoperation concern. Change tracking and performance target derivation would be misplaced in a control module, creating semantic confusion and high coupling to safety policy logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single authoritative owner of the bidirectional traceability matrix. It maintains the matrix as a living artifact, tracks changes on both input and target sides, and derives performance targets as a consistent view. External modules (Tasking, Vehicle, Teleoperation) push updates and consume derived targets via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry). This design achieves high cohesion (all traceability logic in one place) and low coupling (external modules interact only through published interfaces).
containment_risks: Risk: Tasking module may attempt to directly modify performance targets without going through the traceability matrix, bypassing change tracking. Mitigation: Enforce that all target updates flow through Safety Services via Tasking interface.

Risk: Vehicle or Teleoperation may cache stale performance targets if SafetyAlert notifications are delayed or lost. Mitigation: Implement periodic re-sync of targets via Telemetry or explicit query interface.

Risk: Change propagation latency in the matrix may cause transient misalignment between worksite inputs and derived targets. Mitigation: Define acceptable propagation SLAs and implement eventual consistency guarantees.

Risk: Matrix complexity may grow unbounded if worksite inputs are not normalized or deduplicated. Mitigation: Establish input schema and validation rules within Safety Services to prevent matrix bloat.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criteria require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained history of baselines/calibration updates. Safety Services module can directly satisfy this via the bidirectional traceability matrix artifact, with change tracking providing audit trail. Verification is concrete: inspect matrix contents, validate traceability links, confirm change history logs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR-CR-NGHE-005.1 is unambiguously allocated to Safety Services as the single authoritative owner. The module boundary explicitly encompasses safety plan traceability, performance target derivation, change tracking, and matrix maintenance. External modules (Tasking, Vehicle, Teleoperation) have well-defined input/output roles via published interfaces.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B correctly addresses FR-CR-NGHE-005.1. The bidirectional traceability matrix is a sound logical solution for deriving performance requirements from worksite mission context. The DP specifies: (1) matrix structure linking inputs to targets, (2) living artifact maintenance, (3) change tracking on both sides, (4) derived view generation. This directly satisfies the functional requirement to derive performance targets from worksite inputs. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: RISK-1 (HIGH): Tasking module may bypass Safety Services and directly modify performance targets, circumventing change tracking and traceability. MITIGATION: Enforce strict interface contract—all target updates must flow through Tasking->Safety Services inbound interface; implement read-only target consumption via SafetyAlert outbound interface.

RISK-2 (MEDIUM): Vehicle and Teleoperation modules may cache stale performance targets if SafetyAlert notifications are delayed, lost, or not subscribed. MITIGATION: Implement periodic re-sync mechanism (e.g., Telemetry push or explicit query API) with defined SLA for target freshness; add version/timestamp to all emitted targets.

RISK-3 (MEDIUM): Change propagation latency in the matrix may cause transient misalignment between worksite inputs and derived targets, violating acceptance criterion for documented traceability. MITIGATION: Define acceptable propagation SLA (e.g., <5 seconds); implement eventual consistency guarantees with explicit 'in-flight' state tracking; log all intermediate states.

RISK-4 (MEDIUM): Matrix complexity may grow unbounded if worksite inputs (safety plan sections, policy clauses, operational parameters) are not normalized or deduplicated. MITIGATION: Establish input schema validation and deduplication rules within Safety Services; implement matrix size monitoring and alerts; define input normalization procedures.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural, cohesive owner of the bidirectional traceability matrix. All core responsibilities (matrix maintenance, change tracking, target derivation) are co-located in a single module, achieving high cohesion. External modules interact only through well-defined interfaces: Tasking (input updates), Telemetry (validation data), SafetyAlert (target violations and change notifications). Minor diffusion occurs at interface boundaries (Tasking must push updates, Telemetry must push validation data, Vehicle/Teleoperation consume targets), but this is appropriate and expected for a service-oriented architecture. No scattered responsibilities or duplicated logic detected. The module boundary is semantically sound: safety governance and traceability are inherently Safety Services concerns, not Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE orchestration concerns.

recommended_revisions: REVISION-1: Formalize the Tasking->Safety Services interface contract. Specify: (a) input schema for worksite updates (safety plan sections, policy clauses, operational parameters), (b) validation rules to prevent malformed or duplicate inputs, (c) explicit rejection/error handling for invalid updates, (d) audit logging of all input mutations.

REVISION-2: Define SafetyAlert notification protocol. Specify: (a) target change event schema (old target, new target, reason, timestamp), (b) subscription/delivery guarantees (at-least-once, exactly-once, or best-effort), (c) retry/backoff strategy for failed deliveries, (d) consumer acknowledgment requirements.

REVISION-3: Establish Telemetry->Safety Services validation interface. Specify: (a) operational state data schema, (b) validation logic to detect drift between derived targets and actual behavior, (c) threshold/tolerance for acceptable drift, (d) escalation procedure when drift exceeds tolerance.

REVISION-4: Document the bidirectional traceability matrix structure. Specify: (a) input entity types (safety plan section ID, policy clause ID, operational parameter name), (b) target entity types (performance metric name, threshold, unit), (c) link cardinality (1:1, 1:N, N:M), (d) change history schema (timestamp, actor, old value, new value, reason).

REVISION-5: Define performance target derivation algorithm. Specify: (a) rules for combining multiple inputs into a single target, (b) conflict resolution when inputs contradict, (c) default/fallback targets when inputs are incomplete, (d) version/baseline management for target sets.

REVISION-6: Add explicit acceptance test for traceability. Verify: (a) every performance target in the matrix has at least one traceable input, (b) every worksite input has at least one derived target, (c) change history is complete and auditable, (d) matrix is queryable and exportable in documented format (e.g., CSV, JSON).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The bidirectional traceability matrix DP is logically sound and well-allocated to Safety Services. However, the gate analysis reveals four medium-to-high diffusion risks at module boundaries (Tasking bypass, stale target caching, change propagation latency, matrix complexity growth) and six recommended revisions that are architectural in nature—they define interface contracts, notification protocols, validation schemas, and matrix structure. These revisions are prerequisites for safe decomposition: without formalized interface contracts and validation rules, sub-level FRs (e.g., 'Maintain bidirectional traceability', 'Detect target drift', 'Propagate target changes') would inherit ambiguous boundary conditions and lack clear acceptance criteria. Decomposing now would create orphaned sub-FRs with unclear ownership and interface semantics. ZIGZAG_DOWN with mandatory revisions ensures that the next level of functional decomposition operates within a well-defined, risk-mitigated architectural context.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-CR-NGHE-005.1.1: Validate and ingest worksite mission inputs (safety plan sections, policy clauses, operational parameters) from Tasking module with schema enforcement and deduplication.
FR-CR-NGHE-005.1.2: Derive performance targets from validated worksite inputs using conflict-resolution rules and fallback logic, maintaining version/baseline history.
FR-CR-NGHE-005.1.3: Maintain bidirectional traceability links between worksite inputs and derived performance targets with complete change history and audit logging.
FR-CR-NGHE-005.1.4: Detect and escalate drift between derived performance targets and actual operational behavior (via Telemetry validation data) within defined tolerance thresholds.
FR-CR-NGHE-005.1.5: Publish performance target changes and violations to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via SafetyAlert notifications with guaranteed delivery and version tracking.
FR-CR-NGHE-005.1.6: Provide queryable and auditable access to the traceability matrix and change history for compliance verification and forensic analysis.

revise.why: REVISION-1 through REVISION-6 from gate analysis must be incorporated into DP-B before decomposition. Specifically: (1) Formalize Tasking->Safety Services input interface contract with schema, validation, and error handling (REVISION-1). (2) Define SafetyAlert notification protocol with event schema, delivery guarantees, and retry strategy (REVISION-2). (3) Establish Telemetry->Safety Services validation interface with drift detection thresholds (REVISION-3). (4) Document bidirectional traceability matrix structure including entity types, cardinality, and change history schema (REVISION-4). (5) Specify performance target derivation algorithm with conflict resolution and fallback logic (REVISION-5). (6) Add explicit acceptance test for traceability completeness and auditability (REVISION-6). These revisions transform DP-B from a high-level concept ('bidirectional traceability matrix') into a detailed design specification with unambiguous interface contracts, validation rules, and acceptance criteria. Without these revisions, sub-level FRs will lack clear boundary conditions and verification hooks.

assumptions: Tasking module will reliably push worksite input updates to Safety Services via a well-defined inbound interface; no direct target modification by Tasking is permitted.
Telemetry module will push operational state data to Safety Services for validation; Safety Services is responsible for detecting drift, not Telemetry.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules will subscribe to SafetyAlert notifications and respect published performance targets; they will not cache targets indefinitely without re-sync.
Change propagation latency in the bidirectional traceability matrix is acceptable if bounded by an explicit SLA (e.g., <5 seconds); eventual consistency is tolerable for non-critical updates.
Worksite inputs (safety plan sections, policy clauses, operational parameters) can be normalized and deduplicated using a defined schema; unbounded matrix growth is preventable via input validation.
Performance target derivation rules (conflict resolution, fallback logic, version management) can be formalized and implemented deterministically within Safety Services.
Audit logging and change history tracking are feasible within Safety Services without significant performance degradation; acceptable log retention and query performance are achievable.
open_risks: RISK-1 (HIGH): Tasking module may bypass Safety Services and directly modify performance targets, circumventing change tracking and traceability. MITIGATION: Enforce strict interface contract; implement read-only target consumption via SafetyAlert; add runtime checks to detect unauthorized mutations.

RISK-2 (MEDIUM): Vehicle and Teleoperation modules may cache stale performance targets if SafetyAlert notifications are delayed, lost, or not subscribed. MITIGATION: Implement periodic re-sync mechanism with defined SLA; add version/timestamp to all emitted targets; implement consumer acknowledgment.

RISK-3 (MEDIUM): Change propagation latency in the matrix may cause transient misalignment between worksite inputs and derived targets. MITIGATION: Define acceptable propagation SLA; implement eventual consistency guarantees with explicit 'in-flight' state tracking; log all intermediate states.

RISK-4 (MEDIUM): Matrix complexity may grow unbounded if worksite inputs are not normalized or deduplicated. MITIGATION: Establish input schema validation and deduplication rules; implement matrix size monitoring and alerts; define input normalization procedures.

RISK-5 (MEDIUM): Conflict resolution algorithm for deriving targets from multiple inputs may be ambiguous or non-deterministic, leading to inconsistent target derivation. MITIGATION: Formalize conflict resolution rules in REVISION-5; implement deterministic algorithm with explicit precedence/priority rules; add unit tests for conflict scenarios.

RISK-6 (LOW): Traceability matrix query performance may degrade as matrix size grows; compliance verification and forensic analysis may become slow. MITIGATION: Implement indexing on input/target entity IDs; define acceptable query latency SLA; monitor query performance and optimize as needed.
evidence_needed: Formalized Tasking->Safety Services input interface contract (schema, validation rules, error handling, audit logging).
SafetyAlert notification protocol specification (event schema, delivery guarantees, retry strategy, consumer acknowledgment).
Telemetry->Safety Services validation interface specification (operational state data schema, drift detection logic, tolerance thresholds, escalation procedure).
Bidirectional traceability matrix structure documentation (entity types, cardinality, change history schema, queryability).
Performance target derivation algorithm specification (conflict resolution rules, fallback logic, version/baseline management).
Explicit acceptance test for traceability (target-to-input coverage, input-to-target coverage, change history completeness, auditability).
Interface contract enforcement mechanism (e.g., API gateway, message broker validation, runtime checks) to prevent Tasking bypass.
SafetyAlert subscription and delivery mechanism (e.g., pub-sub broker, webhook, polling) with defined SLA and retry strategy.
Periodic re-sync mechanism for Vehicle and Teleoperation modules (e.g., Telemetry push, explicit query API) with version/timestamp tracking.
Input schema validation and deduplication rules within Safety Services (e.g., JSON schema, normalization procedures).
Matrix size monitoring and alerting mechanism to detect unbounded growth.
Change propagation SLA definition and monitoring (e.g., <5 seconds for critical updates).
Eventual consistency guarantees and 'in-flight' state tracking for transient misalignment scenarios.
Unit tests for conflict resolution algorithm covering multiple input scenarios and edge cases.
Performance benchmarks for traceability matrix queries (latency, throughput) as matrix size grows.
